**Noscapine**, also known as **narcotine**, **nectodon**, **nospen**, **anarcotine** and (archaic) **opiane**, is a [benzylisoquinoline](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline "Benzylisoquinoline") [alkaloid](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") of the phthalideisoquinoline structural subgroup, which has been isolated from numerous species of the [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae") (poppy) family. It lacks effects associated with [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") such as [sedation](/wiki/Sedation "Sedation"), [euphoria](/wiki/Euphoria "Euphoria"), or [analgesia](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") (pain-relief) and lacks addictive potential.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Noscapine is primarily used for its [antitussive](/wiki/Antitussive "Antitussive") (cough-suppressing) effects.

## Medical uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Medical uses")]

Noscapine is often used as an [antitussive](/wiki/Antitussive "Antitussive") medication.[[2]](#cite_note-2) A 2012 Dutch guideline, however, does not recommend its use for [acute](/wiki/Acute_(medicine) "Acute (medicine)") [coughing](/wiki/Cough "Cough").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Side effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Side effects")]

* [Nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea")
* [Vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting")
* [Loss of coordination](/wiki/Loss_of_coordination "Loss of coordination")
* [Hallucinations](/wiki/Hallucinations "Hallucinations") (auditory and visual)
* [Loss of sexual drive](/wiki/Loss_of_sexual_drive "Loss of sexual drive")
* [Swelling of prostate](/wiki/Swelling_of_prostate "Swelling of prostate")
* [Loss of appetite](/wiki/Loss_of_appetite "Loss of appetite")
* [Dilated pupils](/wiki/Dilated_pupils "Dilated pupils")
* [Increased heart rate](/wiki/Increased_heart_rate "Increased heart rate")
* [Shaking](/wiki/Tremor "Tremor") and [muscle spasms](/wiki/Muscle_spasms "Muscle spasms")
* [Chest pain](/wiki/Chest_pain "Chest pain")
* Increased [alertness](/wiki/Alertness "Alertness")
* Increased [wakefulness](/wiki/Wakefulness "Wakefulness")
* Loss of [stereoscopic vision](/wiki/Stereoscopic_vision "Stereoscopic vision")

## Interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Interactions")]

Noscapine can increase the effects of centrally sedating substances such as [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)") and [hypnotics](/wiki/Hypnotic "Hypnotic").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The drug should not be taken with [monoamine oxidase inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitor "Monoamine oxidase inhibitor") (MAOIs), as unknown and potentially fatal effects may occur.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Noscapine should not be taken in conjunction with [warfarin](/wiki/Warfarin "Warfarin") as the [anticoagulant](/wiki/Anticoagulant "Anticoagulant") effects of warfarin may be increased.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Biosynthesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Biosynthesis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Noscapine_Biosynthesis.tif/lossy-page1-500px-Noscapine_Biosynthesis.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Noscapine_Biosynthesis.tif)

Noscapine biosynthesis in *P. somniferum*

The biosynthesis of noscapine in *[P. somniferum](/wiki/P._somniferum "P. somniferum")* begins with [chorismic acid](/wiki/Chorismic_acid "Chorismic acid"), which is synthesized via the [shikimate pathway](/wiki/Shikimate_pathway "Shikimate pathway") from [erythrose 4-phosphate](/wiki/Erythrose_4-phosphate "Erythrose 4-phosphate") and [phosphoenolpyruvate](/wiki/Phosphoenolpyruvate "Phosphoenolpyruvate"). Chorismic acid is a precursor to the amino acid [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"), the source of nitrogen in [benzylisoquinoline alkaloids](/wiki/Benzylisoquinoline_alkaloids "Benzylisoquinoline alkaloids"). Tyrosine can undergo a [PLP](/wiki/Pyridoxal_phosphate "Pyridoxal phosphate")-mediated transamination to form [4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid](/wiki/4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic_acid "4-hydroxyphenylpyruvic acid") (4-HPP), followed by a [TPP](/wiki/Thiamine_pyrophosphate "Thiamine pyrophosphate")-mediated decarboxylation to form [4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde](/wiki/4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde "4-hydroxyphenylacetaldehyde") (4-HPAA). Tyrosine can also be hydroxylated to form [3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine](/wiki/3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine "3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine") (DOPA), followed by a PLP-mediated decarboxylation to form [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"). [Norcoclaurine synthase](/wiki/(S)-norcoclaurine_synthase "(S)-norcoclaurine synthase") (NCS) catalyzes a [Pictet-Spengler reaction](/wiki/Pictet-Spengler_reaction "Pictet-Spengler reaction") between 4-HPAA and dopamine to synthesize (*S*)-[norcoclaurine](/wiki/Norcoclaurine "Norcoclaurine"), providing the characteristic benzylisoquinoline scaffold. (*S*)-Norcoclaurine is sequentially 6-*O*-methylated (6OMT), *N*-methylated (CNMT), 3-hydroxylated (NMCH), and 4′-*O*-methylated (4′OMT), with the use of cofactors [S-adenosyl-methionine](/wiki/S-adenosyl-methionine "S-adenosyl-methionine") (SAM) and [NADP](/wiki/NADP "NADP")+ for methylations and hydroxylations, respectively. These reactions produce (*S*)-[reticuline](/wiki/Reticuline "Reticuline"), a key branchpoint intermediate in the biosynthesis of benzylisoquinoline alkaloids.[[6]](#cite_note-doi:10.1007/s11101-019-09644-w-6)

The remainder of the noscapine biosynthetic pathway is largely governed by a single biosynthetic 10-gene cluster.[[7]](#cite_note-pmid22653730-7) Genes comprising the cluster encode enzymes responsible for nine of the eleven remaining chemical transformations. First, berberine bridge enzyme (BBE), an enzyme not encoded by the cluster, forms the fused four-ring structure in (*S*)-[scoulerine](/wiki/Scoulerine "Scoulerine"). BBE uses O2 as an oxidant and is aided by cofactor [flavin adenine dinucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") (FAD). Next, an *O*-methyltransferase (SOMT) methylates the 9-hydroxyl group. Canadine synthase (CAS) catalyzes the formation of a unique C2-C3 methylenedioxy bridge in (*S*)-canadine.[[8]](#cite_note-pmid24316226-8) An *N*-methylation (TNMT) and two hydroxylations (CYP82Y1, CYP82X2) follow, aided by SAM and O2/NADPH, respectively. The C13 alcohol is then acetylated by an acetyltransferase (AT1) using [acetyl-CoA](/wiki/Acetyl-CoA "Acetyl-CoA"). Another [cytochrome P450](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450 "Cytochrome P450") enzyme (CYP82X1) catalyzes the hydroxylation of C8, and the newly formed hemiaminal spontaneously cleaves, yielding a tertiary amine and aldehyde. A methyltransferase heterodimer (OMT2:OMT3) catalyzes a SAM-mediated *O*-methylation on C4′.[[9]](#cite_note-pmid29723437-9) The *O*-acetyl group is then cleaved by a carboxylesterase (CXE1), yielding an alcohol which immediately reacts with the neighboring C1 aldehyde to form a hemiacetal in a new five-membered ring. The apparent counteractivity between AT1 and CXE1 suggests that acetylation in this context is employed as a protective group, preventing hemiacetal formation until the ester is enzymatically cleaved.[[10]](#cite_note-pmid25485687-10) Finally, an [NAD](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide")+-dependent [short-chain dehydrogenase](/wiki/Short-chain_dehydrogenase "Short-chain dehydrogenase") (NOS) oxidizes the hemiacetal to a lactone, completing noscapine biosynthesis.[[6]](#cite_note-doi:10.1007/s11101-019-09644-w-6)

## Mechanism of action

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mechanism of action")]

Noscapine's antitussive effects appear to be primarily mediated by its [σ–receptor](/wiki/Sigma_receptor#The_σ_receptor "Sigma receptor") agonist activity. Evidence for this mechanism is suggested by experimental evidence in rats. Pretreatment with [rimcazole](/wiki/Rimcazole "Rimcazole"), a σ-specific [antagonist](/wiki/Receptor_antagonist "Receptor antagonist"), causes a dose-dependent reduction in antitussive activity of noscapine.[[11]](#cite_note-pmid9232674-11) Noscapine, and its synthetic derivatives called noscapinoids, are known to interact with microtubules and inhibit cancer cell proliferation [[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Structure analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Structure analysis")]

The [lactone](/wiki/Lactone "Lactone") ring is unstable and opens in basic media. The opposite reaction is presented in acidic media. The bond (C1−C3′) connecting the two optically active carbon atoms is also unstable. In aqueous solution of [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") and heating it dissociates into [cotarnine](/w/index.php?title=Cotarnine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cotarnine (page does not exist)") (4-methoxy-6-methyl-5,6,7,8-tetrahydro-[1,3]dioxolo[4,5-*g*]isoquinoline) and [opic acid](/w/index.php?title=Opic_acid&action=edit&redlink=1 "Opic acid (page does not exist)") (6-formyl-2,3-dimethoxybenzoic acid). When noscapine is reduced with [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc")/[HCl](/wiki/HCl "HCl"), the bond C1−C3′ saturates and the molecule dissociates into [hydrocotarnine](/w/index.php?title=Hydrocotarnine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hydrocotarnine (page does not exist)") (2-hydroxycotarnine) and [meconine](/w/index.php?title=Meconine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Meconine (page does not exist)") (6,7-dimethoxyisobenzofuran-1(3*H*)-one).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

Noscapine was first isolated and characterized in chemical breakdown and properties in 1803 under the denomination of "Narcotine"[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-pmid27984164-14) by Jean-Francois Derosne, a French chemist in Paris. Then [Pierre-Jean Robiquet](/wiki/Pierre-Jean_Robiquet "Pierre-Jean Robiquet"), another French chemist, proved narcotine and morphine to be distinct alkaloids in 1831.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Finally, Pierre-Jean Robiquet conducted over 20 years between 1815 and 1835 a series of studies in the enhancement of methods for the isolation of [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), and also isolated in 1832 another very important component of raw opium, that he called [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"), currently a widely used opium-derived [compound](/wiki/Compounding "Compounding").

## Society and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Society and culture")]

### Recreational use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recreational use")]

There are anecdotal reports of the recreational use of [over-the-counter drugs](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug") in several countries,[[16]](#cite_note-16) being readily available from local pharmacies without a prescription. The effects, beginning around 45 to 120 minutes after consumption, are similar to [dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") and [alcohol intoxication](/wiki/Alcohol_intoxication "Alcohol intoxication"). Unlike dextromethorphan, noscapine is not an [NMDA receptor antagonist](/wiki/NMDA_receptor_antagonist "NMDA receptor antagonist").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Noscapine in heroin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Noscapine in heroin")]

Noscapine can survive the manufacturing processes of [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") and can be found in street heroin. This is useful for law enforcement agencies, as the amounts of contaminants can identify the source of seized drugs. In 2005 in [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), the average noscapine concentration was around 8%.[[18]](#cite_note-pmid16265967-18)

Noscapine has also been used to identify drug users who are taking street heroin at the same time as prescribed [diamorphine](/wiki/Diamorphine "Diamorphine").[[19]](#cite_note-pmid16367984-19) Since the diamorphine in street heroin is the same as the pharmaceutical diamorphine, examination of the contaminants is the only way to test whether street heroin has been used. Other contaminants used in [urine](/wiki/Urine "Urine") samples alongside noscapine include [papaverine](/wiki/Papaverine "Papaverine") and [acetylcodeine](/wiki/Acetylcodeine "Acetylcodeine"). Noscapine is metabolised by the body, and is itself rarely found in urine, instead being present as the primary metabolites, [cotarnine](/w/index.php?title=Cotarnine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cotarnine (page does not exist)") and [meconine](/w/index.php?title=Meconine&action=edit&redlink=1 "Meconine (page does not exist)"). Detection is performed by [gas chromatography-mass spectrometry](/wiki/Gas_chromatography-mass_spectrometry "Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry") or liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LCMS) but can also use a variety of other analytical techniques.

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Research")]

### Clinical trials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Clinical trials")]

The efficacy of noscapine in the treatment of certain [hematological](/wiki/Hematological "Hematological") malignancies has been explored in the clinic.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Polyploidy](/wiki/Polyploidy "Polyploidy") induction by noscapine has been observed *in vitro* in human lymphocytes at high dose levels (>30 μM); however, low-level systemic exposure, e.g. with cough medications, does not appear to present a genotoxic hazard. The mechanism of [polyploidy](/wiki/Polyploidy "Polyploidy") induction by noscapine is suggested to involve either chromosome [spindle apparatus](/wiki/Spindle_apparatus "Spindle apparatus") damage or cell fusion.[[22]](#cite_note-pmid1800895-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Noscapine biosynthesis reconstitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Noscapine biosynthesis reconstitution")]

Many of the enzymes in the noscapine biosynthetic pathway was elucidated by the discovery of a 10 gene "operon-like cluster" named HN1.[[7]](#cite_note-pmid22653730-7) In 2016, the biosynthetic pathway of noscapine was reconstituted in yeast cells,[[24]](#cite_note-24) allowing the drug to be synthesised without the requirement of harvest and purification from plant material. In 2018, the entire noscapine pathway was reconstituted and produced in yeast from simple molecules. In addition, protein expression was optimised in yeast, allowing production of noscapine to be improved 18,000 fold.[[25]](#cite_note-25) It is hoped that this technology could be used to produce pharmaceutical alkaloids such as noscapine which are currently expressed at too low a yield *in plantae* to be mass-produced, allowing them to become marketable therapeutic drugs.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Anticancer derivatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Anticancer derivatives")]

Noscapine is itself an [antimitotic agent](/wiki/Antimitotic_agent "Antimitotic agent"), therefore its analogs have great potential as novel anti-cancer drugs.[[27]](#cite_note-pmid19149691-27) Analogs having significant [cytotoxic](/wiki/Cytotoxic "Cytotoxic") effects through modified [1,3-benzodioxole](/wiki/1,3-benzodioxole "1,3-benzodioxole") moiety have been developed.[[28]](#cite_note-pmid34159741-28) Similarly, *N*-alkyl amine, 1,3-diynyl, 9-vinyl-phenyl and 9-arylimino derivatives of noscapine have also been developed.[[29]](#cite_note-pmid32608323-29)[[30]](#cite_note-pmid34051055-30)[[31]](#cite_note-pmid33991960-31)[[32]](#cite_note-pmid33687299-32) Their mechanism of action is through [tubulin inhibition](/wiki/Mitotic_inhibitor "Mitotic inhibitor").[[33]](#cite_note-pmid31951171-33)

### Anti-inflammatory effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Noscapine&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Anti-inflammatory effects")]

Various studies have indicated that noscapine has [anti-inflammatory](/wiki/Anti-inflammatory "Anti-inflammatory") effects and significantly reduces the levels of proinflammatory factors such as [interleukin 1β](/wiki/Interleukin_1%CE%B2 "Interleukin 1β") (IL-1β), IFN-c, and IL-6. In this regard, in another study, Khakpour et al. examined the effect of noscapine against carrageenan-induced [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation") in rats. They found that noscapine at a dose of 5 mg/kg body weight in three hours after the injection has the most anti-inflammatory effects. Moreover, they showed that the amount of inflammation reduction at this dose of noscapine is approximately equal to [indomethacin](/wiki/Indometacin "Indometacin"), a standard anti-inflammatory medication. Furthermore, Shiri et al. concluded that noscapine prevented the progression of [bradykinin](/wiki/Bradykinin "Bradykinin")-induced inflammation in the rat's foot by antagonising [bradykinin receptors](/wiki/Bradykinin_receptor "Bradykinin receptor"). In addition, Zughaier et al. evaluated the anti-inflammatory effects of [brominated](/wiki/Brominated "Brominated") noscapine. The brominated form of noscapine has been shown to inhibit the secretion of the [cytokine](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine") [TNF-α](/wiki/TNF-%CE%B1 "TNF-α") and the [chemokine](/wiki/Chemokine "Chemokine") [CXCL10](/wiki/CXCL10 "CXCL10") from [macrophages](/wiki/Macrophage "Macrophage"), thereby reducing inflammation without affecting macrophage survival. Furthermore, the bromated derivative of noscapine has about 5 to 40 times more potent effects than noscapine. Again, this brominated derivative also inhibits [toll-like receptors](/wiki/Toll-like_receptor "Toll-like receptor") (TLR), TNF-α, and [nitric oxide](/wiki/Nitric_oxide "Nitric oxide") (NO) in human and mouse macrophages without causing toxicity.
